question: Compare the Ch.01 overview of NuScale safety (no LBLOCA, passive ECCS) with Ch.05's ECCS valve specifications and Figure 5.1-3 RCS schematic.

nuscale_ch01_0026.title: Fission Product Removal and Control Systems
nuscale_ch01_0026.summary: The only credited fission product removal and control system in the NuScale design is the containment vessel (CNV) working with the containment isolation system. Fission product control is achieved inherently through the passive depletion of the CNV atmosphere, ensuring containment of radioactive materials.

nuscale_ch01_0069.title: Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams
nuscale_ch01_0069.summary: This section describes the use of simplified electrical, instrumentation and control (I&C), and mechanical drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) to visually clarify design information. It lists the relevant diagrams and legends used for both I&C and piping and instrumentation diagrams, and notes that site-specific diagrams and legends will be provided by COL applicants referencing the NuScale design certification.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and is responsible for transferring heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation. It includes components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and various valves, and features systems for removing noncondensible gases and maintaining the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary to prevent radionuclide release. The RCS interfaces with several other systems, including emergency core cooling and chemical control, to ensure safe and efficient operation.

nuscale_ch01_0063.title: Emergency Core Cooling System Valve Design Certification Application Demonstration Test
nuscale_ch01_0063.summary: This section describes the demonstration test program for the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) valves in the NuScale reactor design. The test program aimed to verify the functional performance of the ECCS valves—including their operation at reactor pressures and temperatures, and in borated coolant—by using a test setup that replicated the actual valve configuration and included unique features like the inadvertent actuation block (IAB). Testing was conducted at a specialized facility to ensure the valves met design requirements under realistic conditions.

nuscale_ch01_0064.title: NuScale Test and Inspection Plans
nuscale_ch01_0064.summary: This section describes NuScale’s test and inspection plans for key reactor components, including confirmation of control rod drive shaft operability and control rod assembly (CRA) drop performance. It details a test program for the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) valves, demonstrating their functionality under reactor operating conditions and in borated coolant, with testing conducted at a specialized facility to validate the unique design features and operational reliability of the ECCS valves.

nuscale_ch01_0025.title: Control Room Habitability System
nuscale_ch01_0025.summary: The Control Room Habitability System (CRHS) is a passive safety feature designed to protect plant operators from accidental releases of toxic or radioactive gases by supplying clean, compressed air to the main control room (MCR), even during power loss. The system maintains positive pressure in protected areas and has enough air storage to support the MCR for at least 72 hours after an incident.

nuscale_ch01_0024.title: Emergency Core Cooling System
nuscale_ch01_0024.summary: The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) in the NuScale design provides passive removal of decay heat during a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) using three reactor vent valves and two recirculation valves, all of which remain closed during normal operation. During an emergency, the system vents steam into containment for condensation and recirculates water to the core, with cooling achieved passively through heat conduction to the reactor pool.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance across various power levels by establishing steady-state parameters such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures, using both software and manual calculations. It details how uncertainties and design considerations are addressed to ensure efficient operation, maximize electrical generation, and maintain safety margins, particularly emphasizing the low pressure losses that support the plant’s natural circulation design.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the NuScale pressurizer, including the RPV upper shell, baffle plate, heater bundles, and associated components. It specifies the types of alloys and stainless steels used, their compliance with relevant BPVC material specifications, and highlights corrosion protection measures such as weld cladding and material compatibility.

nuscale_ch01_0765.title: DSRS 15.5.1-15.5.2, Rev 0: Chemical and Volume Control System Malfunction That Increases Reactor Coolant InventoryII.3 Evaluation Model must be an approved model or be justified.
nuscale_ch01_0765.summary: This section reviews NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for various accident scenarios, focusing on the Chemical and Volume Control System malfunction that increases reactor coolant inventory. It notes which Standard Review Plan (SRP) criteria are applicable, partially applicable, or not applicable to NuScale’s design, and confirms that the frequency classification for this event as an Anticipated Operational Occurrence (AOO) conforms to requirements. Additionally, it specifies that the evaluation model used must be either an approved model or be adequately justified.

nuscale_ch05_0013.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0013.summary: This section lists references and standards related to the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report. It includes guidelines and reports from the Electric Power Research Institute and the American Society of Mechanical Engineers, as well as specific ASME code cases relevant to reactor component materials, quality assurance, and inspection procedures.

nuscale_ch05_0011.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Inservice Inspection and Testing
nuscale_ch05_0011.summary: This section describes the materials and standards for threaded fasteners in the reactor coolant pressure boundary, specifying the use of Alloy 718 for compatibility and stress corrosion cracking mitigation. It also outlines that preservice and inservice inspection and testing of pressure-retaining components are conducted according to the latest applicable ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) requirements, as mandated by federal regulations, with inspection intervals and code editions specified in the inspection program.

nuscale_ch01_0411.title: SRP 9.5.5, Rev 3: Emergency Diesel Engine Cooling Water SystemAll (II.1 thru II.7)Compliance with GDC 2, GDC 4, GDC 5, GDC 17, GDC 44, GDC 45, and GDC 46
nuscale_ch01_0411.summary: The NuScale plant design does not include or require safety-related emergency diesel generators, and therefore the associated systems (fuel oil storage, cooling water, starting, lubrication, and air intake/exhaust) and their compliance with various General Design Criteria (GDC) are not applicable. The plant does not rely on AC or DC power for the performance of its safety functions.

edges.description: Section A describes the functional role and configuration of the ECCS valves in NuScale's passive safety approach, while Section B provides the detailed drawings and schematics (including Figure 5.1-3) that visually clarify and specify the design and arrangement of these ECCS components.

Section A describes the passive ECCS function and its reliance on reactor vent valves, while Section B specifies the operational role of these same valves in both normal and emergency conditions, directly connecting the overview to the technical specifications.

Section A provides a general overview of the ECCS's passive safety function and valve configuration, while Section B details the specific design and test results of the ECCS valves referenced in Section A, connecting the overview to technical validation.

Section A provides a general overview of the ECCS's passive safety function and valve configuration, while Section B details the specific test results and technical validation of the ECCS valve operability, connecting the overview to demonstrated compliance.

Section A provides visual and schematic details (such as P&IDs and figures) that clarify and support the system descriptions and operational functions outlined in Section B, specifically regarding the ECCS valve arrangements and RCS configuration.
Section A provides schematic and diagrammatic context for the ECCS valve locations and system layout referenced in Section B's discussion of ECCS valve demonstration testing and operability.

Section A provides visual and schematic clarification of system components, including ECCS valves, which are further detailed in Section B through test and inspection plans confirming their operability and compliance with safety requirements.

Section A provides the drawings and schematics (such as P&IDs) that visually clarify the ECCS valve arrangement and operation described in Section B's textual overview of the passive ECCS function and configuration.

Section A describes the function and role of the reactor vent valves (RVVs) in the ECCS during emergency operation, while Section B provides test results confirming the operability and performance of these ECCS valves, specifically the RVVs, thereby supporting the claims made in Section A about their function.

Section A describes the function and placement of ECCS reactor vent valves (RVVs) in the RCS, while Section B discusses test results and certification demonstrations specifically for these ECCS valves, directly connecting the system overview to the technical validation of the same components.

Section A describes the function and operation of the reactor vent valves (RVVs) during ECCS operation, while Section B provides an overview of the ECCS design and its passive safety function, with both sections connecting through the role and operation of the RVVs in emergency core cooling.

Section A describes the ECCS valve demonstration test results, while Section B outlines the test and inspection plans that ensure those ECCS valves meet operational requirements, thus Section B provides the procedural context for verifying the results presented in Section A.

Section A describes the ECCS valve demonstration test results that confirm operability, while Section B outlines the ECCS's passive safety function and valve configuration, thus Section A provides evidence supporting the functional claims made in Section B.

Section A discusses test results confirming the operability and performance of ECCS valves, while Section B describes the ECCS's passive safety function and identifies the specific valves involved, thus Section A provides evidence that supports the functional claims made in Section B about the ECCS.

Section A's overview of the ECCS in Ch.01 is supported and specified in greater detail by Section B's technical valve specifications and the RCS schematic in Ch.05, connecting the high-level safety claims to concrete design features.

Section A describes the functional overview and components of the ECCS, while Section B provides the detailed drawings and schematics (such as Figure 5.1-3) that visually specify and clarify the ECCS components and their arrangement referenced in Section A.

Section A describes the passive ECCS function and identifies the reactor vent valves as key components, while Section B specifies the operational role of these same valves (RVVs) in both normal and ECCS emergency conditions, directly connecting the overview to detailed system specifications.

Section B describes the passive ECCS design and valve configuration referenced in Section A's overview of NuScale's safety approach, specifically supporting the claim of passive safety and no LBLOCA scenario.

Section A discusses the system evaluation and performance criteria for NuScale's safety systems, while Section B provides the detailed drawings and schematics (such as Figure 5.1-3) that visually clarify and specify the components and configurations referenced in Section A's overview.

Section A discusses system evaluation criteria for optimal performance, while Section B describes specific RCS features including reactor vent valves (RVVs) that are part of ECCS operation, connecting the general system overview to the detailed valve specifications relevant to safety analysis.

Section B provides a general overview of the ECCS function and configuration (including passive safety and valve arrangement) as described in Ch.01, while Section A specifies detailed material and component information for pressurizer elements referenced in the broader system context.

Section A references specific component material details and their locations, while Section B explains that figures (such as P&IDs) visually clarify these components' arrangement and function, connecting textual descriptions to schematic representations.

Section A details the materials and components of the pressurizer, some of which are referenced in Section B's system description of how the RCS and ECCS valves function to maintain system integrity and remove gases, thus Section A provides specific component information that supports the general system functions described in Section B.

Section A references regulatory requirements and evaluation models for safety analysis, while Section B specifies the design and operation of the NuScale ECCS valves that are intended to satisfy those regulatory requirements for passive decay heat removal.

Section A references regulatory requirements and conformance for safety systems, while Section B provides the detailed drawings and schematics (such as the RCS schematic) that visually clarify and specify the design information discussed in Section A.

Section B provides specific system design details (e.g., ECCS valve functions and RCS degasification) that support and illustrate the general safety overview and regulatory conformance claims made in Section A regarding NuScale's passive ECCS and RCS integrity.

Section A provides a system-level evaluation of NuScale's safety systems, referencing the steam generators and their role in decay heat removal, while Section B specifies the detailed materials and design features of the pressurizer, which is a component within the reactor coolant system described in Section A.

Section A describes the system evaluation and operational criteria for NuScale's safety systems, while Section B discusses regulatory conformance regarding technical specifications such as primary-to-secondary leak rates, connecting them through the need for design parameters in A to satisfy the regulatory requirements in B.

Section A provides detailed material specifications for pressurizer components referenced in Section B's discussion of regulatory conformance and evaluation models for reactor coolant system safety, connecting the physical design to the regulatory requirements.

Section A lists references that provide technical standards and guidelines relevant to the integrity and quality assurance of reactor coolant boundary components, which underpin the design and operation of the ECCS described in Section B.

Section A lists references that provide standards and guidelines cited in the NuScale FSAR, while Section B explains that figures (such as P&IDs) visually clarify design information referenced in the text, indicating that the references in Section A may underpin the technical content depicted in Section B's diagrams.

Section A lists references that establish the technical and quality assurance standards (such as ASME NQA-1 and EPRI guidelines) which underpin the system descriptions and operational claims made in Section B regarding the RCS and ECCS valve functions.

Section A specifies the material and design standards for threaded fasteners in the reactor coolant pressure boundary, which are critical components ensuring the integrity and operability of the ECCS valves described in Section B.

Section A describes the material specifications and regulatory compliance for threaded fasteners in the reactor coolant system, while Section B explains that figures (such as piping and instrumentation diagrams) visually clarify component design details referenced in the text, including those fasteners.

Section A details the material and inspection requirements for threaded fasteners that are part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, which Section B describes as essential for maintaining the integrity of the RCPB during both normal and emergency ECCS operations.

Section A explains that NuScale's safety design does not rely on emergency diesel generators or AC/DC power for safety functions, while Section B describes the passive ECCS valve arrangement that enables decay heat removal without electrical power, thus Section B's design details support and exemplify the safety claims made in Section A.

Section A explains that NuScale's safety design does not rely on emergency diesel generators or their cooling systems, while Section B provides the detailed drawings and schematics (such as P&IDs) that visually clarify and support this design approach.

Section A explains that NuScale's safety design does not rely on emergency diesel generators or their cooling systems due to its passive ECCS, while Section B describes how the ECCS operates passively via reactor vent valves without dependence on AC or DC power, thus Section B's system details satisfy and exemplify the claims made in Section A about passive safety and lack of reliance on active power sources.

Section A cites industry standards and guidelines (such as ASME NQA-1 and EPRI documents) that are directly relevant to the material selection and quality assurance requirements described in Section B for threaded fasteners in the reactor coolant pressure boundary.

Section A lists references supporting the technical basis for the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary, while Section B documents the NuScale design's conformance with regulatory criteria, specifically noting that passive safety features eliminate the need for emergency diesel systems, thus connecting the technical references in A to the regulatory compliance statements in B.

Section A describes the material and regulatory compliance of RCS boundary fasteners, while Section B states that NuScale's design does not require safety-related emergency diesel generators or their cooling systems, both reflecting the plant's reliance on passive safety features and lack of dependence on active systems.
